task: Write a Python function count_distinct_pairs(nums, target) returning how many distinct unordered value pairs in the list sum to target, each distinct pair counted once no matter how many times its values repeat: [1, 3, 1, 3] with target 4 has exactly one such pair. An empty list returns 0. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code correctly counts distinct unordered pairs that sum to the target, handles edge cases such as an empty list, but could be more concise.
trace: The approach involves iterating through the given list and keeping track of the occurrences of each number using a dictionary (dct). For each number, if its complement (target - num) exists in the dictionary, we increment our counter. To handle possible repeated values, we make sure to check for the existence of the number in the dictionary before adding it or updating its count. This way, when a distinct pair is found, it will be counted only once no matter how many times its values repeat. An edge case that this solution handles is an empty list, where the function returns 0.
